segments.s254-g153: بدین کار is a prepositional phrase meaning 'regarding/with this matter' (classical contraction of به + این); it appears before the rest of the clause. بیژن names the topic of the سخن (Bijan = the subject of the tale). سخن ساختم is a verb phrase: سخن (object 'speech/words') + ساختم, past 1st person singular of ساختن used here in the sense 'compose/utter' — together 'I composed/said a speech about Bijan.' The overall word order reflects Persian V-final structure with an initial prepositional phrase.
segments.s254-g154: بپیران is a prepositional phrase 'to the elders' formed with prefixed ب (be-, = به) + پیران (pl. of پیر 'elders'); و coordinates the next noun. گودرز is a proper name appended as a second indirect object. پرداختم is past 1st person singular of پرداختن: here it means 'I delivered/presented (it)/I turned it over to' and takes the previously mentioned سخن as its implicit object. The prepositional phrases precede the verb, and the subject 'I' is omitted but encoded by the -م ending on both verbs.
segments.s254-g129: This is a short noun phrase listing gifts. یکی functions as an indefinite numeral/pronoun 'one (of them)' and comes before the items. تاج is the first item 'a crown'; the conjunction و links it to ده بدره دینار, where ده is the numeral 'ten' and بدره modifies دینار (a poetic epithet literally 'full-moon' describing the dinars). نیز at the end means 'also' and emphasizes that these were among other gifts.
segments.s254-g130: A continuation of the gift-list connected by و. پرستنده here is a nominal item in the list (poetically 'a worshipper' or devoted follower, i.e. a person given as part of the gifts). The following و links فرش 'carpet' and then و هرگونه چیز 'and every kind of thing' (هرگونه = 'any/ every kind', چیز = 'thing'). The whole phrase is enumerative; there is no finite verb in this line because the verb of giving is supplied by the larger context.
segments.s254-g131: به بیژن بفرمود is a light-verb construction meaning 'he commanded/said to Bijan'. به + proper name marks the addressee. بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he said/ordered'. کاین is the fused relative/ demonstrative 'that/this' introducing the clause, and خواسته is 'the request/petition' (object of the following infinitival/imperative clause).
segments.s254-g132: ببر is the imperative 'take!' addressed to Bijan (here transitive; the direct object is the خواسته named in the previous segment). سوی is a preposition 'toward/ to'. ترک is the noun 'Turk' and روان‌کاسته is a postpositive adjective (compound) meaning 'downcast / reduced in spirit' and so modifies ترک. Word order: imperative + directional phrase + modified noun; the object is understood from the previous clause.
segments.s254-g133: This is a pair of prohibitions. مگوی is the classical negative imperative 'do not say / do not tell'. سردش is 'cold (speak) to him' where سرد (stern/cold) is followed by the possessive enclitic ش 'his' (= 'to him' in context). The first part, برنجش مفرسا, is idiomatic: برنجش here refers to 'resentment/anger' (a poetic form related to رنجش) and مفرسا conveys 'excessive/overly' — together the sense is 'do not make/raise his resentment excessively' (i.e., 'do not inflame his anger'). Because this is a classical poetic line some morphemes are compressed or archaic in form.
segments.s254-g134: نگر is the imperative 'look!' or 'watch!'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that' (here meaning 'so that you may see'). چه is the interrogative 'what', آوردی is used here as a tense/aspect form meaning 'you bring / you will bring' (what you will bring), او را is the definite object marker with the pronoun 'him' (او + را) and بروی is the subordinate verb 'you go' (subjunctive/optative form used in purpose/time clauses). The clause order is somewhat inverted: 'look so that what you bring him when you go (be seen)'.
segments.s254-g135: This is an imperative/advice to the addressee: تو (you, emphatic) + با او (with him) sets accompaniment. جهان را is the object 'the world' (here idiomatically 'life' or 'the world of affairs'), بشادی is an adverbial phrase 'with joy / happily', and گذار is the verb 'let pass; spend' (imperative sense: 'pass/spend [your life/time]'). The construction means roughly 'Spend your life with him in happiness' or 'Pass through the world happily with him.'
segments.s254-g136: نگه کن is a two-word imperative 'keep/watch!' (نگه + کن 'do'). بدین is the fused prepositional form 'به + این' = 'over/with this' (i.e. 'with this' or 'over this'), and گردش روزگار is a genitive phrase 'the turning/cycle of the times' (گردش = turning/circuit, روزگار = time/fate). Together: 'Keep watch over this turning of the times' (i.e. be mindful of the changing fortunes of the world).
segments.s254-g81: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' (not simile); از + خوان‌سالار is a prepositional phrase 'from the chief steward/table of the steward'; برخاستند is a 3rd person plural past verb 'they rose/rose up' matching the plural subject implied. Word order is conjunction → prepositional phrase → verb, typical in narrative clauses.
segments.s254-g82: نشستنگه is a noun 'seat/place for sitting' (poetic compound form); می‌بیاراستند is a prefixed present/imperfective form (می‑) with past-narrative sense here, 3rd person plural 'they arranged/ornamented'. Together they mean 'they arranged the seats'; the verb agrees with the plural subject 'they'.
segments.s254-g83: A nominal juxtaposition: فروزنده مجلس is an epithet 'the illuminator/one who lights the court/assembly' (فروزنده modifying مجلس). و simply links the next noun: میگسار 'cupbearer'. There is no finite verb in this line; these are nominative appositive attendants listed by role.
segments.s254-g84: نوازنده چنگ is a noun phrase 'the player/musician of the harp' (instrument governed by the following noun). با پیشکار is a prepositional phrase 'with an attendant/assistant'. The order 'instrumental noun + با + attendant' lists companions/roles; again no finite verb is expressed.
segments.s254-g85: همه 'all/everyone' is the general subject; بر سران is a locative prepositional phrase 'upon the heads'; افسران گران is a noun + adjective 'helmets (افسران) precious/heavy (گران)'. In this poetic line the copula is omitted: the phrase describes what was on everyone's heads.
segments.s254-g86: بزر is an attributive adjective 'great' modifying the following idea; اندرون پیکر is a compound 'the inner part/body' (in poetry the ezâfe can be omitted), and از + گوهران means 'of/from gems'—here 'made of gems' or 'whose inner part was of gems'. The segment supplies the material/inner description of the helmets.
segments.s254-g87: همه رخ 'every/each face' is the topic; چو introduces a simile 'like/as', so چو دیبای رومی برنگ = 'like Roman brocade in color' (دیبای رومی = Rûm/Byzantine brocade). The simile modifies رخ (face) and uses postpositive color phrase برنگ 'in hue'.
segments.s254-g88: خروشان is an adjective/participle 'roaring/tumultuous' used predicatively; ز introduces the cause 'from/by (the) harp' (ز چنگ). The coordination و links a second noun phrase پریزاده چنگ — here 'a harp (or sound) born of a fairy' (پریزاده modifying چنگ). The whole segment says the faces were tumultuous because of the harp and the extraordinary (fairy-born) harp.
segments.s254-g1: چو is a poetic conjunction meaning 'when' introducing a temporal clause. آگاهی آمد is a short verb phrase 'news/information came' (verb after subject is common in older/poetic Persian). بشاه is the dative 'to the king' (ب + شاه) placed after the verb by poetic inversion, and دلیر is a postpositive epithet 'brave' modifying شاه.
segments.s254-g2: که introduces a subordinate (content/relative) clause 'that/who'. از بیشه is the prepositional phrase 'from the grove'. پیروز is an adjectival epithet 'victorious' modifying شیر; برگشت is the past verb 'returned' (poetic word order puts adjective before verb and noun after), and شیر here means 'lion'.
segments.s254-g3: چو again = 'when'. بیژن is the personal name 'Bijan'. شد here means 'became/was' (copular/past sense) and combines with رها to mean 'was freed' or 'became free'. از بند و زندان is a compound prepositional phrase 'from fetters and prison' (بند = fetter).
segments.s254-g4: ز is the poetic form of از 'from'. بند again = 'bond/fetter'. بداندیش is an adjective 'ill‑minded / malicious' describing the agent, and نراژدها here functions as a proper name (the dragon/monster) that held him — the whole phrase means 'from the bonds of the ill‑minded Neražd'.
segments.s254-g5: سپاهی 'army/troops' is the subject. ز توران = 'from Turan' (source). بهم here conveys 'into confusion / in disorder' (they were scattered/together/broken up), and برشکست is a past verb 'broke/was shattered' describing the troops' defeat (poetic order places adverbial/particle before verb).
segments.s254-g6: همه لشکر دشمنان = 'the whole army/host of the enemies' (همه quantifies لشکر). کرد پست is a causative/resultative construction: کرد 'made/did' + پست 'low' meaning 'he humbled / put to low estate' the enemy host; the verb کرد takes the object 'همه لشکر دشمنان'.
segments.s254-g7: بشادی is an adverbial noun 'with joy / joyfully'. به پیش here means 'in the presence of / before' (پیش = before/in the presence of) and جهان‌آفرین is 'the World‑Creator / Creator' (a title for God); the whole phrase indicates the setting of their action (in joyful homage before God).
segments.s254-g8: بمالید is a past verb 'they rubbed' (or 'they applied/pressed'); روی و کله 'face and head' are the direct objects; بر زمین = 'upon the ground'. The verb plus objects expresses the ritual act of pressing/rubbing face and head on the earth (an act of rejoicing or prostration).
segments.s254-g9: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when', introducing a temporal clause. The two names are joined by و 'and', and آگهی (information/news) is the object placed before the past plural verb یافتند ('they found/received'), yielding 'when Goudarz and Giv learned the news.'
segments.s254-g10: سوی is a preposition 'toward/to' taking شاه پیروز ('the victorious king') as its object; پیروز is an epithet attached to شاه. بشتافتند is the past plural verb 'they hurried/ran', so the clause means 'they hurried toward the victorious king.'
segments.s254-g11: This is two coordinated V‑final poetic clauses: برآمد خروش literally 'arose a cry' (verb before noun) and بیامد سپاه 'came the host/army' (again verb then noun). The sequence reports that a shout rose and the army arrived.
segments.s254-g12: تبیره‌زنان is the subject 'the axe‑wielders/axemen', برگرفتند is the past plural verb 'they took up' or 'they assumed/took to' and راه is the object 'the road/way' — together 'the axe‑wielders took the road' (i.e. set out).
segments.s254-g13: This is a compact noun phrase: دمنده 'the blower/trumpeter' followed by modifiers دمان گاودم (the 'hem/pendant of a cow‑tail') and the prepositional phrase بر درش 'upon his gate' (در 'gate/door' with the 3rd‑person possessive suffix ش). The verb is omitted here and appears in the next segment (poetic ellipsis).
segments.s254-g14: برآمد is the past 'arose'; خروشیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive used substantively 'roaring' or 'a cry', and از لشکرش 'from his army' marks the source. Together: 'a roar arose from his army.'
segments.s254-g15: سیه کرده is a perfect/participial phrase 'made black' (lit. 'having made black') modifying میدانش 'his field' (so: 'his field, made black'). اسبان 'horses' follows and بسم is an archaic adverb 'بس' meaning 'enough/abundant', here indicating great numbers — 'horses aplenty' or 'to overflowing.'
segments.s254-g16: همه شهر 'the whole city' is the subject; آوای 'the sound/voice' is the nominal predicate, and رویینه‌خم ('armour‑clad') qualifies the people producing that sound. There is no copula; the construction means 'the whole city was filled with the sound of the armoured (men).'
segments.s254-g41: بر + noun marks motion onto or onto the backs of something here: بر اسبان = 'onto (the) horses'. نشستند is a past‑tense, 3rd‑person plural perfect of نشستن 'to sit/mount', so بر اسبان نشستند = 'they mounted (their) horses'. یکسر is an adverb meaning 'all at once/altogether' and modifies the whole verb phrase; مهان (guests/visitors) is the noun modified by یکسر (i.e. 'the guests all together').
segments.s254-g42: This clause names who approached the king. گرازان is a collective noun (here a troop/company), بنزدیک is a preposition/adverb meaning 'near/close to', followed by the title شاه جهان 'King of the World' (شاه modified by جهان as an epithet). Word order is Subject–predicate omitted (the verb is in the previous clause); the whole phrase functions as a continuation: 'the troop drew near the King of the World.'
segments.s254-g43: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. نزدیک شهر is a locative phrase 'near the city' (نزدیک modifies the noun شهر). جهاندار شاه is a title in apposition: جهاندار (sovereign) qualifies شاه (king), so the phrase reads 'the sovereign king'. The clause sets the time/place: 'When the sovereign king drew near the city...'.
segments.s254-g44: فرازآمد is a past verb meaning 'rose up' or 'advanced' and here denotes a troop's movement. آن is a demonstrative/determiner 'that/the'. گرد in this poetic register denotes a band/group (preceding the epithet), and لشکرپناه is a compound epithet 'protector of the host' (لشکر = host/army, پناه = refuge/protector). Together: 'that band, the protector of the host, advanced.' Note the verb precedes its nominal subject in this poetic inversion.
segments.s254-g45: پذیره here functions as a participial/noun element 'reception/being received' and with the auxiliary/particle شدش yields a passive sense 'he was received' (شدش = 'it happened to him/was done to him'). نامدار جهان is an epithet 'renowned of the world' modifying the following title. The phrase thus means 'he was received by the world‑renowned...'.
segments.s254-g46: This is a pair of appositive titles in a genitival/appositive series: نگهدار ایران = 'guardian/preserver of Iran' and شاه مهان = 'lord/king of guests' (مهان used as an epithet). The conjunction و links the two titles; both function as the agent/host who receives the person in the previous segment.
segments.s254-g47: چو is 'when'; رستم is the subject. بفر here is an archaic contracted prepositional element meaning 'toward' or 'to the direction of' (literally 'بِ ‌فر/به‌ طرف'); it introduces the phrase جهاندار شاه 'the sovereign king' as the target of Rostam's glance. The whole phrase = 'When Rostam looked toward the sovereign king.'
segments.s254-g48: نگه کرد is a light‑verb construction: نگه (a noun/verb stem 'look/gaze') + کرد (simple past 'did') = 'he looked'. کآمد (it came/appeared) is a simple past intransitive meaning 'came forward/appeared'. پذیره here is 'a reception/welcoming party' and براه 'on the road/along the way'. Together: 'he looked, and what appeared was a welcoming party on the road' (natural: 'a welcoming party appeared along the road').
segments.s254-g105: همه (subject) = 'everyone/they all'. پیش is a preposition meaning 'before / in the presence of' and takes the following NP شاه جهان ('the King of the World') as its object; کدخدای is the direct object (no extra postposition here) and is placed after the locative phrase in the free, poetic word order.
segments.s254-g106: بیاورد is a past-tense perfect (he/they brought). و کردند continues in the past ('and they did / they caused'); یک سر here is an adverbial phrase ('یک‌سر' = utterly / entirely) modifying کردند; بپای is an archaic/poetic verbal form (ب + پاییدن) meaning roughly 'to keep/hold/keep in place' or 'to persist', so کردند یک سر بپای = 'they held him entirely / kept him fast'.
segments.s254-g107: همه is the plural subject 'all/they'. رستم زابلی را is the definite/marked object (را) — 'Rostam, the Zâbeli' — and سپرد is the simple past of سپردن ('to entrust / hand over'). In classical narration the agent ('they') is generic and the direct object is explicitly marked with را.
segments.s254-g108: زمین را ببوسید is a straightforward past clause: زمین (the ground) + را (object marker) + ببوسید (he kissed). و links this clause to برخاست گرد, where برخاست is the past of برخاستن ('he rose / stood up / departed') and گرد is an adverb/particle meaning 'about / around' or here idiomatically 'and set off / went about' — together: 'he kissed the ground and rose and departed'.
segments.s254-g109: بسربر is a fused locative phrase (ب + سر + بر) meaning 'upon the head / on the head'; نهاد is the past of نهادن ('he placed / put'), so the clause is 'he placed that Kâlah-ı Kiyân (the Kayan hat) on his head.' Note the classical word order: locative adverbial + verb + definite object.
segments.s254-g110: ببست is past tense of بستن in an emphatic/poetic form here meaning 'he bound / fastened'. آن کیانی ('that Kayan/royal') modifies کمر ('girdle / waist'); برمیان is a locative phrase 'in the middle / around' (here 'round his waist'). Literally: 'he fastened that Kayan girdle around his middle.'
segments.s254-g111: ابر here functions as an honorific/intensifier before شاه (classical poetic usage: 'the great/illustrious king'). کرد آفرین is a light verbal phrase 'he made/sounded “Afarin”' — i.e. 'he praised / said “bravo”.' و برفت is an archaic past form meaning 'and he left / departed' (برفت = برگشت/رفت in classical diction).
segments.s254-g112: ره (راه) 'the way / course' + سیستان را (object-marked place 'Sistan') is the direct object of بسیچید, past of بسیچیدن 'to muster / gather (troops)'. تفت here stands as a final word that in context names an action/place or is a poetic intensifier; grammatically the clause says 'he mustered the way/forces of Sistan.' The sequence is compact and typical of epic diction: verb after object phrases and an uninflected place/particle at the end.
segments.s254-g25: The verb برفتند (past, 3rd plural) precedes its complements in the common epic inversion: 'they departed'. لشکر (army/host) is the semantic subject/agent, and گروها گروه is a plural adverbial expression meaning 'group by group' or 'troop by troop' (repetition is a poetic intensifier). Word order here is verb + subject + adverbial.
segments.s254-g26: This clause uses شد (past of شدن) as a copula meaning 'became' with کوه as the predicate nominative: 'the earth became (a) mountain.' ز گردان بکردار is a causal/agentive prepositional phrase 'from/because of the troop of the bəkardār (noble/valiant),' modifying the change expressed by شد. Note the poetic verblessness between the cause and result is resolved by شد as the linking verb.
segments.s254-g27: چو آمد is a temporal conjunction + past verb meaning 'when there came/when ... appeared.' پدیدار (literally 'appearance' or 'one who appears') functions as the subject that follows the verb, a common poetic order (verb before subject). از انبوه نیو is a prepositional phrase 'out of the crowded mass' specifying source; انبوه is used here in its 'dense' sense.
segments.s254-g28: پیاده شد is a periphrastic verb phrase meaning 'became on foot' → 'dismounted' or 'got off (their mounts).' شد is the light verb and پیاده its predicate adjective/adverb. از باره introduces source/instrument ('from the saddle' or 'from their burden') and is followed by the names گودرز و گیو, the agents who perform the action.
segments.s254-g29: ز اسب (lit. 'from the horse') is a prepositional phrase marking source, followed by اندرآمد, here used as a past verb in the sense 'to come down / alight' (poetic usage for 'dismount'). جهان‌پهلوان (the title) appears after the verb as the topical subject — inversion typical in epic style.
segments.s254-g30: بپرسیدش is the past verb 'asked him' with the object clitic -ش attached (3rd‑person). از introduces the topic or object of questioning 'about/concerning,' followed by رنج‌دیده 'the suffering/battle‑worn' as the noun phrase. گوان is a small poetic particle/ending here that broadens or pluralizes the reference ('those like that' / 'such ones'); it modifies رنج‌دیده rather than the verb.
segments.s254-g31: آفرین کرد is a light‑verb construction meaning 'made praise' → 'praised' or 'cried "bravo".' The prefixed برو here functions as an interjection ('bravo!' / 'well done!') directly linked to آفرین; گودرز و گیو after the verb are the agents (typical epic order: verb before subject).
segments.s254-g32: که‌ای here combines the relative/particle که with the vocative ای ('O'), functioning as an address 'O you who are...'. نامبردار and سالار are epithets in apposition ('renowned' and 'commander'), and نیو is the following nominal identifier (here treated as a name/epithet). The phrase as a whole is a vocative tag addressing the person praised in the previous segment.
segments.s254-g121: ازان is the fused poetic form of و + از آن meaning “and/from that”; تنگ زندان is a noun phrase (adjective تنگ ‘narrow/tight’ before noun زندان ‘prison’), joined by و to رنج زوار. Here رنج زوار is a nominal phrase meaning severe/exhausting suffering (زوار used in a physical/wear sense). The whole chunk names the thing he spoke about.
segments.s254-g122: فراوان modifies the verbal phrase that follows and means “much/at length”; سخن گفت is a common collocation (‘to say/speak words’ → ‘to speak’), with the verb گفت in the past. با شهریار is the prepositional phrase ‘with/to the king’ (با + object). Word order is adverb – noun phrase – verb – prepositional phrase, giving “spoke plentifully (the) words to the king.”
segments.s254-g123: وزان is و + ازان, ‘and from that / and because of that’; گردش روزگاران is a genitive construction where گردش (‘turn, course’) is qualified by روزگاران (‘of the times’). بد is a postpositive adjective (‘bad, ill’) describing the course. Together: ‘that bad turning/course of the times.’
segments.s254-g124: همه داستان is the direct object ‘the whole story’; پیش خسرو is a locative/prepositional phrase ‘in the presence of Khosrow / before Khosrow’. The verb بزد is a poetic/archaic use here (literal senses listed are ‘strike, smite’) that in epic usage often means ‘utter/relate’ (i.e. he ‘struck out’ the story = he told it). Word order places object and prepositional phrase before the terse past verb.
segments.s254-g125: بپیچید is the perfective/past form of پیچیدن with an archaic/preverbal ب- (here ‘he turned/pleaded/pleaded earnestly’ in narrative); و links the clause to the next. بخشایش (object) precedes the verb آورد (past ‘brought, obtained’) — Persian allows object-before-verb order — so بخشایش آورد سخت reads ‘he brought/obtained pardon harshly/firmly’ where سخت modifies how strongly/decisively the pardon was secured (i.e. he obtained strong/ample clemency).
segments.s254-g126: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from/because of’; درد و غم is the coordinated reason ‘pain and sorrow’. دخت (maiden) appears before the predicate گم بوده بخت, an inversion typical of epic style: گم بوده (had been lost) modifies بخت (fortune), so the clause says literally ‘by pain and sorrow the maiden’s fortune had been lost.’
segments.s254-g127: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past form often used of a ruler’s speech or command here meaning ‘he ordered/commanded’. صد جامه (numeral + noun) is the direct object ‘a hundred garments’, and دیبای روم specifies their material and origin (‘brocade/silk of Rome’), with the origin word روم postpositive.
segments.s254-g128: همه پیکرش is a possessive phrase ‘all of his/her body’ (پیکرش = پیکر + -ش). The following list گوهر و زر و بوم names the adornments: jewels, gold, and (textually) bوم — here meaning a fine fabric/cloth — placed after the noun phrase; the verb of being/adorning is omitted (elliptical), a common poetic compression: ‘her whole body (was) jewels, gold and cloth.’
segments.s254-g145: جهان را: 'جهان' (the world) is marked by را, which topicalizes/definitizes it ('as for the world'). ز کردار بد: ز + noun phrase gives cause/ground ('because of/because of the bad conduct'); the adjective بد follows کردار as usual. شرم نیست: شرم (shame) is the nominal predicate and نیست negates it ('there is no shame'). Overall: 'As for the world, because of bad conduct there is no shame.'
segments.s254-g146: کسی را: کسی is topicalized by را ('as for anyone / no one in particular'). برش: here functions idiomatically as a short/pronominal item ('for it / regarding it') rather than its literal senses listed; it connects to the following nouns. آب و آزرم نیست: آب is used figuratively (purity/life/honor) and آزرم is an archaic/obscure word for dignity/honor; نیست negates the whole ('there is no [such] honor/esteem'). The clause thus says that no one cares or has honor in relation to it.
segments.s254-g147: همیشه: adverb 'always'. بهر نیک و بد: بهر here means 'for/with respect to' and links to the coordinated nouns نیک و بد ('good and bad'). دسترس: predicate meaning 'within reach / accessible' — the phrase says that both good and bad are always accessible/attainable.
segments.s254-g148: ولیکن: adversative 'but/yet'. نجوید: present tense negative-form idea 'does not seek' (here third-person general statement; classical negative-present form). خود آزرم کس: خود ('one's own / oneself') modifies آزرم (honor/dignity); کس ('anyone') is the logical subject, so the clause means 'yet no one seeks his own honor.'
segments.s254-g149: چنینست: a fixed predicate 'such is / thus it is'. کار سرای سپنج: کار 'affair/work' is qualified by the following phrase; سرای here functions as a genitival element ('the house/realm of...') in meaning though the provided lemma senses are verbal — the poet uses سرای + noun as a compound 'the house/realm of X'. سپنج is a poetic epithet (literally 'sponge') used metaphorically to name that place/condition.
segments.s254-g150: گهی ... گهی: repetitive adverb 'sometimes ... sometimes' framing alternating states. ناز و نوش: two nouns linked by و — ناز (pampering/affection, delicate ease) and نوش (delight/drinking/pleasure) form the positive pole. درد و رنج: the parallel negative pole — درد (pain/affliction) and رنج (suffering/hardship). The coordination shows alternation between enjoyment and suffering.
segments.s254-g151: ز بهر درم: a prepositional phrase 'for the sake of a dirham / because of a coin' (ز + بهر = 'for the sake of'). تا نباشی بدرد: تا introduces a result/condition 'if/so that', and نباشی is a second-person negative present/subjunctive form meaning 'you are not' or 'you become not'; بدرد (here) means 'of use / useful'. Together the clause conveys 'if you are of no use for (the sake of) a dirham.'
segments.s254-g152: بی‌آزار بهتر: a comparative statement 'harmless is better' (بی‌آزار = harmless/inoffensive; بهتر = better). دل رادمرد: دل 'heart' qualifies رادمرد (a man of generosity/integrity): 'the generous-hearted man.' The clause as a whole prefers harmlessness (non-harm) in a generous man to being useless for a small sum.
segments.s254-g17: This is a compact nominal phrase describing captive beasts. بربسته is a past‑participle/adjective 'bar‑baste' (tied, restrained) that modifies شیر و پلنگ; it follows the thing it modifies (typical in poetic word order). بیک دست functions adverbially ('with one hand' or 'by one (person)' — an archaic prepositional/agential phrase) and precedes the participial phrase, so the whole chunk reads as ‘(held) by one hand, tied: the lion and the leopard.’
segments.s254-g18: بزنجیر is an adjectival/prepositional formation meaning 'in chains' or 'chained' and here qualifies the following noun cluster. دیگر modifies سواران ('other/remaining horsemen'), and جنگ is a postpositive attributive noun (genitive/qualifier) 'of war' — together: 'other war‑horsemen in chains.' Word order places the chain‑phrase first, then the noun it describes.
segments.s254-g19: گرازان (literally 'boars') stands before سواران and functions as a simile/adjectival epithet 'boar‑like' modifying 'horsemen'. دمان و دنان are a paired, archaic/poetic plural of epithets or nouns placed after the head noun for emphasis/apposition; their exact lexical meaning is obscure here, so they function as ornamental descriptors following the main noun.
segments.s254-g20: بدندان is an adverbial/prepositional phrase 'with (their) teeth' or 'gnashing (their) teeth'; it governs زمین 'the ground' (i.e. 'gnashing/biting at the earth'). ژنده is an adjective 'ragged' modifying the following image پیلان کنان, where پیلان (from پیل) 'elephants' is used figuratively and کنان is a present‑participle/adverbial 'doing/going' — together 'ragged, moving/advancing like elephants.'
segments.s254-g21: بپیش سپاه is a prepositional phrase meaning 'before/toward the army' (بپیش = 'in front of / before'); اندرون follows and means 'within/inside', so the whole phrase situates the instruments: 'in front of the army, inside (the ranks), trumpets and drums.' بوق و کوس are listed as the items found there.
segments.s254-g22: درفش is the subject 'banner/standard'. از پس پشت is a compound locative/prepositional phrase 'from/behind the back (rear) of …' where از = 'from', پس = 'after/behind', and پشت = 'back/rear'; it attaches to the names گودرز و طوس ('Goudarz and Tous') to yield 'the standard behind the backs of Goudarz and Tous.'
segments.s254-g23: پذیره شدن is a verbal noun + infinitive construction meaning 'the receiving / the reception (being made)'; it is used here in a circumstantial clause. پیش پهلو سپاه is a locative/possessive phrase 'in front of the flank of the army' (پیش = 'before/in front of', پهلو = 'flank/side', سپاه = 'the army'). Together: 'with the reception having taken place at the army's flank…'.
segments.s254-g24: بدین گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in this manner / thus' (here formed from the fused b‑ + دین/این in poetry). فرمود is the past tense verb 'he said / commanded'. بیدار شاه is the nominative that follows (epithet + noun) 'the alert/awake king' — literal order is 'thus said the alert king.'
segments.s254-g49: پیاده شد is a short past tense clause: پیاده (on foot) + شد (past of شدن used here as 'became/got' → 'dismounted/went on foot'). The clitic/predicate برد (here a verb meaning 'brought/took' in the context) follows with پیشش 'before him / in his presence' (preposition + third-person enclitic), and نماز is the direct object placed before the following predicate in this poetic line. Note the compact, slightly archaic word order where actions are listed in sequence with و connecting them.
segments.s254-g50: غمی گشته is an adjectival/predicative phrase: غمی 'sorrow/grief' + گشته (perfect/participial sense 'has become' / 'turned') indicating a resultant state. از + رنج و راه دراز gives the cause/qualification: از 'from/because of', رنج 'suffering', و linking to راه دراز 'the long road' (راه = road; دراز = long). The whole phrase modifies or comments on نماز (i.e. the prayer—worn with grief from hardship and a long journey).
segments.s254-g51: جهاندار خسرو is a titled name phrase ('the world-ruler Khosrow'). گرفتش is گرفت (past of گرفتن 'to seize, take') plus the object clitic -ش 'him' (so 'he seized him'). ببر here appears as a short vocative/epithet (babr, 'tiger') or an exclamatory word immediately following the clause; its exact function is poetic (an epithet or addressing/exclaiming).
segments.s254-g52: که‌ای is an exclamatory/relative particle here introducing an exclamation ('what a...'): که + ای producing 'what a' or 'how'. دست مردی is a possessive/attributive phrase: دست 'hand' with مردی meaning 'manliness' or 'manly quality' (so 'a manly hand' / 'the hand of a hero'). و links to جان هنر: جان 'life/soul' + هنر 'skill/virtue' (a poetic collocation: 'the very soul of skill'). The construction is an exclamatory praise following the seizure.
segments.s254-g53: تهمتن is an epithet/name (Tahmatan, 'the mighty one'); سبک دست is a compound adjective meaning 'light-handed' or 'nimble of hand' (سبک = light, دست = hand), and بیژن is the proper name Bijan. گرفت is the past tense verb 'seized/took.' The order is name + descriptive adjective + object + verb, typical of poetic Persian where modifiers precede nouns and the verb closes the clause.
segments.s254-g54: چنانکش is a compact poetic form of چنانکِ which means 'in such a way / so that / thus' and introduces the manner/result clause. ز is the poetic preposition 'from', شاه 'king' and و پدر 'and (his) father' name the sources, and بپذرفت is an older past form (بـ- prefix + پذرفت, 'he accepted/received'). Together it says he received him in that same manner from the king and the father (i.e. accepted/took him from them).
segments.s254-g55: A sequence of three past verbs joined by و: بیاورد (brought), بسپرد (handed over/entrusted — بـ- prefix + سپرد), and بر پای خاست (stood up — بر 'on/upon' + پای 'feet' + خاست 'arose'). The serial verbs narrate successive actions: he brought (the man), handed him over, and then stood up on his feet (i.e. rose).
segments.s254-g56: چنان 'thus/so' links to the previous action; پشت خمیده 'bent back' (پشت = back, خمیده = bent) is the definite object marked by را; کرد is the past of کردن 'to make/do' used causatively ('made straight'), and راست is the result 'straight' (i.e. he straightened that previously bent back). Word order here places the object with its adjective before the verb, as is common in Persian.
segments.s254-g89: طبقهای سیمین is a noun phrase with the plural noun طبقهای (trays/plates) followed by the adjective سیمین (silver). پر از مشک ناب is a predicate adjective phrase where پر (here 'full') takes the preposition از to mean 'full of' and is completed by مشک ناب ('pure musk'). The sequence is a compressed descriptive line typical of poetic Persian: noun + adjective, then 'full of' + content + qualifier.
segments.s254-g90: بپیش introduces a locative phrase 'before/in front of' (ب + پیش). اندرون is the inner‑quarters/inner apartments and is the head of the phrase. آبگیری گلاب is a compound noun where آبگیری ('a place holding water, a basin/pool') is specified by گلاب ('rosewater'); the whole phrase means 'a basin/holding of rosewater' placed before the inner rooms.
segments.s254-g91: همی is an archaic/poetic habitual adverb meaning 'continually' or 'kept on'. تافت is the past tense of tăftan 'to shine' used here in the narrative past with iterative sense due to همی. ازفر شاهنشهی is a genitive/construct: ازفر ('radiance/splendour') qualified by شاهنشهی ('imperial/of kingship'), literally 'the radiance of kingship.'
segments.s254-g92: چو introduces a simile 'like/as'. ماه دو هفته is a compact expression meaning 'the moon of two weeks' (i.e. a moon two weeks on, a particular lunar phase used poetically). ز introduces a source/comparative 'from/of' and سرو سهی 'dark/black cypress' is a poetic image; together the phrase forms a visual simile ('like the moon... from the dark cypress'). Word order is compressed for meter: simile marker + image.
segments.s254-g93: همه is the universal quantifier 'all/everyone' preceding the noun پهلوانان (plural 'champions, heroes'). خسروپرست is a postpositive compound adjective meaning 'king‑worshipping' or 'devotees of the king'; Persian places such compounds after the noun. Together: 'all the king‑worshipping champions.'
segments.s254-g94: برفتند is the simple past 'they went/left' (3rd person plural). The following sequence appears compressed and poetic: زایوان should be read as z + ایوان ('from the iwan/portico') though the lemma given does not match that reading; in classical Persian ز often attaches as a preposition meaning 'from'. سالار مست is a nominal phrase 'the master/chief, drunk' where مست ('drunk') qualifies سالار. In other words, the clause indicates departure with reference to the lord's iwan/porch and his drunken state; the prepositional z is the normal 'from.'
segments.s254-g95: بشبگیر is an adverbial temporal phrase 'at nightfall/by night' (poetic). چون here introduces a temporal/simile clause ('when/just as') and links to the following verb آمد 'came'. بدر ('full moon') is used as a simile or circumstantial image modifying how Rostam arrived: 'Rostam came forth like the full moon.' آمد is the simple past 'he came.'
segments.s254-g96: This is a coordinated descriptive phrase: گشاده‌دل 'open‑hearted, generous' coordinated with a participial construction تنگ بسته کمر 'having the waist/belt tied tight.' In Persian the past participle بسته ('tied/bound') follows the adjective تنگ ('tight') and the noun کمر ('waist/belt') completes the idea — together: 'open‑hearted and with his belt tightly fastened.'
segments.s254-g65: A parallel noun phrase: خنک (adjective) precedes شهر ایران ('the city of Iran'); و links this phrase to فرخ گوان, another nominal phrase. Adjective-before-noun order and the coordination are typical of poetic Persian; ګوان is a descriptive noun following فرخ.
segments.s254-g66: که introduces a relative clause modifying the preceding coordinated nouns. دارند is 3rd-person plural present ('they have'), agreeing with the compound subject. چون تو = 'one like you' (چون = 'like'), and یکی پهلوان ('one hero') is the direct object; Persian places object before verb here.
segments.s254-g67: An abbreviated nominal statement: وزین (archaic/poetic connective 'from/of these' or a coronated particle) links to هر سه ('all three'); برتر 'superior' modifies the coordinated nouns سر و بخت ('head and fortune'). من is the possessor placed after the possessed nouns (poetic word order), and there is no copula expressed.
segments.s254-g68: که introduces the reason/explanatory clause. چون تو is a simile/relative ('one like you'); پرستد is a classical present (with the final -د) meaning 'worships/reveres' and همی adds continuous/habitual sense; تخت من 'my throne' appears before the verb as its object.
segments.s254-g69: به خورشید is a simile/comparative prepositional phrase ('like the sun'); ماند همی is a compound verbal idea with ماند ('last, endure') and the continuous particle همی, giving 'endures continually'. کار تو ('your deed') is the topical object placed after the verb cluster in poetic order.
segments.s254-g70: بگیتی is an adverbial locution 'in the world'; پراگنده modifies کردار ('widespread deed/conduct'), and تو is the possessor placed after the noun phrase. Word order (adjective + noun + possessor) is common in classical poetic Persian.
segments.s254-g71: بگیو is an archaic/poetic imperative/subjunctive form meaning 'say!' or 'speak!'. آنگهی ('thereupon') is an adverb linking to the speech report; the verb گفت ('said') follows, and شاه جهان ('the king of the world') appears after the verb in inverted poetic order.
segments.s254-g72: که introduces the content clause. نیکست is the fused archaic form of نیک است ('it is good/well'); با + کردگارت = 'with your Creator' (کردگار + -ت possessive), and نهان is predicate/adjective 'hidden' — together: 'that it is well hidden with your Creator.'
segments.s254-g137: یکی را is the direct object ('someone'); برآرد is a third-person present/present-subjunctive poetic form of برداشتن/آوردن meaning 'raises' or 'brings up' and takes that object. بچرخ بلند is a nominal/agent phrase ('the lofty Wheel' or 'by the high turning') placed after the verb by poetic inversion: چرخ (the cosmic Wheel/fate) qualified by بلند ('lofty').
segments.s254-g138: ز is the preposition 'from'; تیمار و دردش is a coordinated object/phrase ('care and his pain') with the enclitic pronoun ش marking possession. کند is the third-person singular present of کردن used causatively ('makes/causes'), and بی‌گزند is a predicative adjective meaning 'unharmed' — together: 'makes (him) free from care and pain and unharmed.'
segments.s254-g139: وزانجاش is an adverbial 'from there'/'thence'; گردان is a noun meaning 'the turning' or 'rotation' (here: the turn of fate) serving as the subject/agent. برد is used as the verb 'carried/took' (poetic past of بردن), and سوی خاک is the prepositional phrase 'toward the dust/earth.'
segments.s254-g140: همه جای is a fixed phrase 'everywhere' or 'every place'; بیمست functions as a predicate meaning 'is full of fear' (classical contraction of 'بیم است'). The following و تیمار و باک list additional nouns 'and care and dread' — a nominal series describing the state everywhere.
segments.s254-g141: هم introduces emphasis 'even/also'; آن را که is a relative construction 'that one who/whom'; پرورده is a passive/adjectival participle 'nurtured/reared'; باشد here is the classical subjunctive/correlative copula used in relative clauses ('who has been' / 'who may be'); بناز is a present or jussive verb 'boasts / is proud' — overall: 'even the one whom it has reared and who prides himself.'
segments.s254-g142: بیفگند is a third-person verb 'casts/throws' (poetic form of افکندن); خیره is an adjective/adverb meaning 'stunned/astonished' modifying the person cast; بچاه is the prepositional phrase 'into the well' (بـ + چاه); نیاز 'need' is used as a noun to form the idiom 'the well of need' — 'casts (him), stunned, into the well of want.'
segments.s254-g143: یکی را is the object 'someone'; ز چاه آورد is a verbal clause 'brought from the well' (آورد is past/present poetic of آوردن 'to bring'); سوی گاه is an adverbial phrase 'toward the place/occasion' (گاه here 'place/occasion' in a poetic sense), so: 'it brings someone from the well to the place/assembly.'
segments.s254-g144: نهد is the third-person singular present/past poetic form of نهادن 'to place/set'; بر سرش is the locative 'upon his head' (بر + سر + enclitic ش 'his'); بر ز گوهر کلاه is a displaced noun phrase 'a hat of jewels' where بر ز (poetic for از/of) and گوهر modify کلاه. Word order is inverted: verb — locative — modifier + object.
segments.s254-g113: بزرگان (the noun) is the plural definite/collective 'the great ones' or 'the nobles'; که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. بودند is the past plural copula 'were'. با او بهم is a prepositional phrase 'with him together' (با = with, او = him, بهم = together); word order is noun + relative clause + verb + prepositional phrase, typical for classical Persian where the relative כ-clause follows the head noun.
segments.s254-g114: This is a coordinated list of circumstances introduced without an explicit preposition: برزم و ببزم و بشادی و غم enumerates contrasting situations ('in battle / in banquet / in joy / and in sorrow'). Each item is nominal/adverbial; و is the copulative 'and'. These short nouns/adverbials are elliptical (no case-markers) and function as adverbial complements describing the states in which the nobles accompanied him.
segments.s254-g115: براندازه‌شان is an adverbial/quantifier 'in proportion to them / according to their rank' (براندازه + ـشان). یک بیک هدیه داد is a simple SOV clause: یک بیک 'a bey/chieftain' or 'one bey' (here 'a title/gift for a bey'), هدیه 'a gift' is the object, and داد is the past tense 'gave'. The whole segment means 'he gave a gift appropriate to each of them.'
segments.s254-g116: از ایوان خسرو is a prepositional phrase 'from Khosrow's iwan/hall' (از = from, ایوان = iwan/porch, خسرو = Khosrow). برفتند is past plural 'they departed/they went away'. شاد is a predicate/adverbial 'happy/merry', modifying their departure. Word order: locative/ablative phrase + verb + predicate.
segments.s254-g117: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. از کار کردن is a verbal noun phrase 'from doing the work/after finishing the business' (از + کار کردن). بپردخت is a past form meaning 'he paid/dealt with' — here idiomatically 'had finished/dealt with (his) affairs'. شاه is the subject 'the king'. The clause thus reads 'When the king had dealt with his work'.
segments.s254-g118: برام بنشست بر پیشگاه: بنشست is past 'he sat down / took a seat'. بر پیشگاه is a locative 'upon/at the presence (of the court)'. برام here is an adverbial/archaic locution attached to the verb (in context: 'took his seat'); the exact courteously phrased prepositional nuance is archaic, but functionally the clause means 'he took his seat before the court.'
segments.s254-g119: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning 'he said/commanded' (used for sovereigns to mean 'he commanded/declared'). تا introduces a subordinate clause often with purposive or declarative force here 'that / so that'. بیژن is the proper name 'Bijan'. آمدش is 'came to him/approached him' (آمد + ـش). پیش is 'before/in the presence of'. Together: 'He commanded that Bijan come before him.'
segments.s254-g120: سخن گفت is the verbal phrase 'he spoke / he said' (سخن = speech/words, گفت = spoke). زان is a contracted prepositional/demonstrative 'from that / about that'. رنج و تیمار خویش is a coordinated object 'that suffering and his own care/trouble' (خویش = his own; here خوی + ضمیر ش). The clause means 'he spoke about that suffering and his own troubles.'
segments.s254-g73: This is a relative/clause introducer 'که' (that/who) followed by a locative/agent phrase. 'بر دست رستم' literally 'on/into Rostam's hand' specifies the locus or instrument of the action. 'جهان‌آفرین' (the World‑Creator) stands in front of the verb and functions as the subject; the order (prepositional phrase — subject) is poetic but common in classical Persian.
segments.s254-g74: 'بتو داد' is the core verbal piece: 'داد' (gave) with the indirect object 'بتو' (to you). The noun phrase 'پیروز پور گزین' is the direct object that follows the verb (poetic word order). Here 'پیروز' is adjectival/epithet 'victorious', 'پور' = 'son', and 'گزین' functions as 'chosen/select(ion)' (an epithet), so together they describe the person given.
segments.s254-g75: 'گرفت آفرین' is a fixed poetic expression meaning 'uttered/acquired acclamation' (literally 'took praise'); 'گرفت' is past tense active 'took/held/uttered'. 'گیو' is the subject (the name Giv). 'بر شهریار' marks the target/beneficiary of the acclamation: 'for/upon the king' — prepositional phrase after the verb is idiomatic in classical Persian.
segments.s254-g76: This relative clause introduced by 'که' modifies 'شهریار'. 'شادان' is an adjective 'joyful'. The word printed 'بدی' here functions as an archaic/metre-driven form of the past copula (comparable to 'بودی' = 'was/remained'); because that verbal sense is not listed in the provided senses it is marked with -1. 'تا بود روزگار' = 'as long as the time endured' — 'تا' = 'until/while' and 'بود' is the past tense of 'to be'.
segments.s254-g77: This is a benediction: the final word 'باد' is the optative particle 'may' (not the noun 'wind'). 'سر رستمت' is 'the head of (or head belonging to) Rostam' — in classical Persian names may carry an enclitic; here the phrase means 'Rostam's head'. Adjectives follow the noun: 'جاودان سبز' = 'eternally/ever green'. Word order is noun + possessive/name + adjectives + optative.
segments.s254-g78: Another optative clause: 'دل زال فرخ' = 'the heart of Zal (be) fortunate' — 'فرخ' is an adjective meaning 'fortunate/auspicious'. 'بدو' = 'to him' (dative pronoun) and the following 'باد شاد' repeats the optative: 'may it be / may he be happy.' The sequence places the beneficiary ('to him') before the optative verb as is common in poetry.
segments.s254-g79: 'بفرمود خسرو' is an honorific/periphrastic past often used for royal speech: 'Khosrow commanded/said'. The conjunction 'که' introduces the subordinate clause. 'بنهید' is the present/subjunctive or jussive form of 'نهادن' (to place/set), here meaning 'set up/establish', and 'خوان' is the object 'banquet/table' — so the clause means 'that a banquet be set (up)'.
segments.s254-g80: Imperative/jussive instruction: 'بزرگان برترمنش را' is the direct object (marked by 'را') — 'the great ones of proud/high bearing' (i.e., great nobles). 'بخوان' here is an imperative/subjunctive from 'خواندن' used in the sense 'call/summon (in), read (in)'; in this context it functions as 'summon/bring in'.
segments.s254-g33: This clause has a relatively free (poetic) word order: دلیر (a brave one) is the predicate nominal, followed by a prepositional phrase indicating origin (از تو — 'from you') and the verb گردد (an older/poetic form of 'become', here 'may become' / 'may spring forth'). بهر جای شیر literally 'for/in the place of the lion' uses بهر + جای to mean 'in the stead of' (بهر = 'for/toward', جای = 'place' used here as 'in place of'). The whole is an optative/wish: 'May a brave one of yours become in the lion's place.'
segments.s254-g34: سپهر (the sky/heavens) is the subject; از تو 'with regard to you / from you'; هرگز is the negating adverb 'never'. مگرداد here is an irregular/archaic form (the text's form of a give/grant verb) used negatively with هرگز to mean something like 'will not be sated/give its fill'; سیر means 'satiety' or 'satisfaction'. Together the clause says the heavens will never be satisfied in relation to you (i.e. heaven's favour never exhausted).
segments.s254-g35: ترا is the object/beneficiary pronoun 'to you' (here written as separate ت + را); جاودان باد is an optative formula where باد is the classical optative particle 'may (it) be' and جاودان 'eternal' modifies the following noun; یزدان پناه literally 'God (be) refuge' — the sequence is a prayer/wish: 'May God be an eternal refuge to you.'
segments.s254-g36: بکام تو 'to your desire/pleasure' (بکام = 'pleasing/to one's desire', تو = 'you/your'). گرداد is an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning 'was turned / be made' (here optative/causative sense: 'may be turned/made'); خورشید و ماه are the two subjects/objects (sun and moon) tied together by و. The clause is another blessing: 'May sun and moon be turned to your wish.'
segments.s254-g37: همه here functions adverbially 'entirely/wholly'. بنده کردی is a two-word predicate 'you made (کردی) [me] a servant (بنده)', with تو explicit as the subject 'you'. این دوده را ('this soot' + object marker را) is a humble phrase for the speaker (a self-deprecatory image). Word order is topical/poetic: the object (این دوده را) is placed after the predicate for emphasis.
segments.s254-g38: زتو 'from you' gives the source/agent for the following action. یافتم is the past tense 'I found'. پور گم‌بوده را 'the lost son' (پور = 'son', گم‌بوده = 'lost', را = object marker). The clause states the speaker obtained/found the lost son through (or from) the addressee.
segments.s254-g39: ز indicates origin/agentive cause here ('from' or 'because of'); درد و غمان 'pain and sorrows' is the source/context; رستگان is a plural/perfective participle meaning 'the saved/delivered' used predicatively; تویم is a fused expression meaning 'I am of you / I belong to you' (the poet claims to be among the addressee's saved). The clause is elliptical/poetic: 'By/through pains and sorrows I am among your saved.'
segments.s254-g40: بایران is a locative/poetic adverbial (here indicating campaign/expedition plains or arena — in the lexicon given it is an older/poetic geography-related word). کمربستگان is a participial plural 'the girded ones' i.e. 'those who have girded their loins,' used metaphorically for ready warriors; تویم again expresses 'I am of you / I belong to you.' Together: 'I am one of your girded warriors for the campaigns.'
segments.s254-g57: ازان پس is a fixed time phrase meaning 'after that/thereupon' (ازان + پس). The noun phrase اسیران توران هزار follows classical Persian ordering where the place-name توران modifies اسیران and the numeral هزار comes after to mean 'a thousand captives of Turan.'
segments.s254-g58: بیاورد is the 3rd-person past/narrative ('he/they brought'). بسته is a past-participle/adjectival complement meaning 'bound' (describing the captives) and functions as an object complement ('brought (them) bound'). بر + شهریار marks motion/toward the king ('to the king'), with بر used here in the sense 'toward/to.'
segments.s254-g59: برو آفرین together form an exclamatory phrase ('Bravo!') with برو functioning idiomatically as the opening of the shout and آفرین the acclamation; کرد is the past tense verb 'he said/made' that governs that quoted exclamation. خسرو is the subject (the king, here the proper name Khosrow) and بمهر is an adverbial phrase 'with affection' (بـ + مهر) describing how he spoke.
segments.s254-g60: This is an optative/wish clause introduced by the subordinating كه; جاوید بادا is an inversion meaning 'may (it be) eternal' (بادا = optative 'may it be'), and بکامت سپهر places the beneficiary/goal ('to your satisfaction' + 'heavens') after the verb phrase. The order is slightly archaic/inverted but reads 'May the heavens be eternal to your desire.'
segments.s254-g61: This clause uses an optative flavor: بگذرد (3rd-singular) here functions as 'may ... pass' in a blessing. خنک is an adjective/adverb 'pleasant/cool' placed before the verb; زال کش is a compound agent-noun (زال + کش, with کش as the agentive element '‑kesh/‑kosh' meaning 'slayer of Zāl' or an epithet). روزگار is the noun 'time/days' that is governed by the verb. The word order is somewhat fronted/inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s254-g62: بماند is a 3rd-person form 'may remain' (optative/hope). بگیتی = بـ (archaic prepositional prefix) + گیتی 'in the world/among people'. ت + را together make the object pronoun 'ترا' (you) with را as the definite/object marker; یادگار is the noun 'memento/remembrance' and is the thing that is wished to remain. The clause literally: 'May a remembrance of you remain in the world.'
segments.s254-g63: خجسته is an adjective 'blessed/auspicious' that qualifies the following compound 'بر و بوم زابل' ('land and home of Zabul'); the relative particle كه introduces the following relative clause whose subject begins with شیر ('lion'). Word order places the epithet before the place-name for emphasis.
segments.s254-g64: همی + پروراند is the habitual/presentive construction in epic Persian ('does/ever nurtures' or 'is wont to raise'); گوان here is an archaic/distributive word meaning 'like/also' (used to link the next noun), and و دلیر completes the list ('and the brave'). Together the clause means that Zabul's land nurtures lions and likewise brave men.
segments.s254-g97: بدستوری is an adverbial compound meaning 'by (the) order' and sets the circumstance. بازگشتن is the verbal infinitive 'to return' used here as the main action (classical Persian often uses the infinitive in narrative where English uses a finite past). بجای expresses destination 'to (their) place(s)' and completes the verb phrase 'return to place'.
segments.s254-g98: همی + زد is a poetic past-continuous construction: همی marks ongoing/habitual action and زد (here 'sound/perform; utter') is the verb; the verb precedes its subject in inversion (a common epic word order), so هشیوار is the subject. با introduces accompaniment or association: با شاه رای means 'in/with the king's counsel' (i.e. consulting or in accordance with the king's plan).
segments.s254-g99: یکی here is the indefinite numeral 'one/a certain'. دست جامه is a fixed phrase 'a suit/set of garments' (دست functioning as a unit/measure with جامه). بفرمود is the honorific past 'he commanded/ordered' with the subject شاه placed after the verb (شاه).
segments.s254-g100: گهر بافته is a compound adjective: گوهر 'gem' + بافته (past participle of 'weave') = 'gem-woven' or 'embroidered with jewels' modifying the garment set. با introduces the list of included pieces ('with a robe and a cap'); the coordination is simple noun-and-noun joined by و.
segments.s254-g101: یکی again 'one/a certain'. جام is 'cup/goblet'. پر is the adjective/particle 'full (of)' and directly follows the noun (a common order in Persian: noun + پر +ِ+ object), with گوهر the object of 'full'. شاهوار is an epithet 'regal/kingly' modifying the whole item.
segments.s254-g102: This lists transport: 'a hundred horses and a hundred camels' (numeral implied by the preceding صد). The verbs بزین و ببار are imperative verbs in classical Persian: بزین 'saddle (them)' and ببار 'load/put on (them)' — two commands joined by و. The direct object (the animals) is omitted but understood from context.
segments.s254-g103: دو پنجه is a numeral + noun phrase ('two [units/figures called] پنجه'); پری‌روی is an adjective 'fairy‑faced / beautiful-faced' that follows the noun (typical adjective-after-noun order). بسته کمر is a participial phrase 'with the waist bound/girded' (بسته = 'tied/bound', کمر = 'waist/girdle') and further describes their appearance.
segments.s254-g104: دو پنجه پرستار repeats the numeral + noun pattern: 'two ... attendants/caretakers' (پرستار = attendant). با introduces the instrument/ornament: طوق زر 'a collar/necklet of gold' — طوق (necklet/collar) + زر (gold). The whole phrase lists two attendants who bear golden collars.
